title: PostgreSQL B-tree vs GIN indexes — when to use which
PostgreSQL full-text search with tsvector and ts_query
body:
Full-text search in PostgreSQL requires creating a tsvector column and indexing it with GIN. The tsvector type stores lexemes (normalized words), and tsquery defines the search terms.

```sql
-- Add a generated tsvector column
ALTER TABLE articles ADD COLUMN search_vector tsvector
  GENERATED ALWAYS AS (
    setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(title, '')), 'A') ||
    setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(body, '')), 'B')
  ) STORED;

-- Index it
CREATE INDEX idx_articles_search ON articles USING gin (search_vector);

-- Query with ranking
SELECT title, ts_rank(search_vector, query) AS rank
FROM articles, to_tsquery('english', 'docker & compose') AS query
WHERE search_vector @@ query
ORDER BY rank DESC
LIMIT 20;
```

The `setweight` function assigns weights A-D to different columns so title matches rank higher than body matches. Use `plainto_tsquery` for user input without boolean operators, `phraseto_tsquery` for exact phrase matching, and `websearch_to_tsquery` for Google-style search syntax (available in PG 11+). The generated column approach keeps the vector automatically in sync without triggers.

title: Reading PostgreSQL EXPLAIN ANALYZE output — what the numbers mean
body:
EXPLAIN ANALYZE executes the query and shows actual timing alongside the planner's estimates. Understanding the output is critical for query optimization.

```sql
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS, FORMAT TEXT)
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 42 AND status = 'pending';
```

Sample output:
```
Index Scan using idx_orders_user_status on orders
  (cost=0.42..8.44 rows=1 width=128)
  (actual time=0.023..0.025 rows=3 loops=1)
  Index Cond: (user_id = 42)
  Filter: (status = 'pending')
  Rows Removed by Filter: 12
  Buffers: shared hit=4
Planning Time: 0.112 ms
Execution Time: 0.041 ms
```

Key fields: `cost` is in arbitrary units (first number is startup cost, second is total). `rows` is the planner's estimate vs `actual rows`. When these diverge wildly, run `ANALYZE orders` to update statistics. `Buffers: shared hit` means pages served from cache; `shared read` means disk I/O. `Rows Removed by Filter` indicates the index fetched 15 rows but only 3 survived the filter — a partial index on `(user_id) WHERE status = 'pending'` would eliminate that waste. Watch for `Seq Scan` on large tables, `Sort` without an index, and `Nested Loop` with high loop counts.

title: PostgreSQL jsonb operators and indexing strategies
body:
PostgreSQL provides operators for querying jsonb columns. Choosing the right operator determines whether your GIN index gets used.

```sql
-- Extraction operators
SELECT data->'address' FROM users;          -- returns jsonb
SELECT data->>'name' FROM users;             -- returns text
SELECT data#>'{address,city}' FROM users;    -- nested path -> jsonb
SELECT data#>>'{address,city}' FROM users;   -- nested path -> text

-- Containment (uses GIN index)
SELECT * FROM users WHERE data @> '{"role": "admin"}';

-- Key existence (uses GIN default_ops, NOT jsonb_path_ops)
SELECT * FROM users WHERE data ? 'email';
SELECT * FROM users WHERE data ?| array['email', 'phone'];

-- Two GIN operator classes:
CREATE INDEX idx_data_default ON users USING gin (data);  -- supports @>, ?, ?|, ?&
CREATE INDEX idx_data_path ON users USING gin (data jsonb_path_ops);  -- only supports @>, smaller index
```

Common mistake: using `data->>'status' = 'active'` in WHERE — this extracts text and cannot use GIN. Rewrite as `data @> '{"status": "active"}'` for index usage. If you frequently query a specific key, a B-tree expression index is more efficient: `CREATE INDEX idx_status ON users ((data->>'status'))`. The jsonb_path_ops operator class produces indexes roughly 30% smaller than default but only supports the containment operator `@>`.

title: Zero-downtime PostgreSQL migrations — expand and contract pattern
body:
Zero-downtime migrations require that old and new application code can run simultaneously against the database. The expand-and-contract pattern splits breaking changes into safe phases.

Phase 1 — Expand (backward-compatible):
```sql
-- Add new column as nullable (instant, no lock)
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN display_name text;

-- Backfill in batches (avoid long transactions)
UPDATE users SET display_name = name
WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 10000 AND display_name IS NULL;

-- Create index concurrently (no lock)
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_users_display ON users (display_name);
```

Phase 2 — Migrate application code to write both columns.

Phase 3 — Contract (remove old column):
```sql
-- Add NOT NULL constraint using NOT VALID (instant, validates on new rows only)
ALTER TABLE users ADD CONSTRAINT display_name_nn
  CHECK (display_name IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID;

-- Validate existing rows (takes ShareUpdateExclusiveLock, allows reads/writes)
ALTER TABLE users VALIDATE CONSTRAINT display_name_nn;

-- Drop old column in final deploy
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN name;
```

Critical rules: never add columns with DEFAULT in PG < 11 (rewrites table). Always use `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY`. Never rename columns — add new, migrate, drop old. Set `lock_timeout = '5s'` to avoid blocking if a lock cannot be acquired quickly.

title: PostgreSQL deadlocks — how they happen and how to prevent them
body:
Deadlocks occur when two transactions each hold a lock the other needs. PostgreSQL detects deadlocks after `deadlock_timeout` (default 1s) and kills one transaction.

```
ERROR: deadlock detected
DETAIL: Process 1234 waits for ShareLock on transaction 5678;
  blocked by process 5679.
Process 5679 waits for ShareLock on transaction 5677;
  blocked by process 1234.
HINT: See server log for query details.
```

Common cause — updating rows in different order:
```sql
-- Transaction A                    -- Transaction B
UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal - 10  UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal + 10
WHERE id = 1;                       WHERE id = 2;
-- holds lock on id=1               -- holds lock on id=2

UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal + 10  UPDATE accounts SET bal = bal - 10
WHERE id = 2;                       WHERE id = 1;
-- waits for id=2 -> DEADLOCK       -- waits for id=1 -> DEADLOCK
```

Fix: always acquire locks in a consistent order (e.g., sort by ID ascending). For bulk operations, use `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE ORDER BY id` to lock in deterministic order. Other prevention strategies: keep transactions short, avoid user interaction mid-transaction, use `SKIP LOCKED` for queue-style processing, and set `lock_timeout` so queries fail fast rather than waiting indefinitely. Monitor with `SELECT * FROM pg_locks WHERE NOT granted;`.

title: pgvector HNSW index — setup and tuning for approximate nearest neighbor search
body:
pgvector's HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) index provides fast approximate nearest neighbor search, outperforming IVFFlat for most workloads.

```sql
-- Install extension
CREATE EXTENSION vector;

-- Create table with embedding column
CREATE TABLE documents (
  id serial PRIMARY KEY,
  content text NOT NULL,
  embedding vector(1536)  -- OpenAI ada-002 dimensions
);

-- Create HNSW index (build is slower than IVFFlat, queries are faster)
CREATE INDEX idx_docs_embedding ON documents
  USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops)
  WITH (m = 16, ef_construction = 64);

-- Query with cosine distance
SELECT id, content, embedding <=> $1::vector AS distance
FROM documents
ORDER BY embedding <=> $1::vector
LIMIT 10;

-- Tune search precision at query time
SET hnsw.ef_search = 100;  -- default 40, higher = more accurate but slower
```

Tuning parameters: `m` (default 16) controls connections per node — higher values increase recall and index size. `ef_construction` (default 64) affects build quality — use 100-200 for production. Unlike IVFFlat, HNSW does not require training data and handles inserts without reindexing. For 1M rows with 1536 dimensions, expect ~6GB index size with m=16. Always benchmark with your data: measure recall@10 against exact results from a sequential scan.

title: Drizzle schema push vs migrate — when to use each
body:
Drizzle ORM offers two schema management strategies: `drizzle-kit push` and `drizzle-kit generate` + `drizzle-kit migrate`. They serve different stages of development.

**Push (`drizzle-kit push`):**
```bash
# Compares your schema files against the live database and applies changes directly
npx drizzle-kit push
```
Push reads your TypeScript schema, diffs it against the connected database, and executes ALTER statements immediately. No migration files are generated. Best for: rapid prototyping, local development, hackathons. Drawbacks: no migration history, no review step, cannot customize generated SQL, dangerous for production.

**Generate + Migrate:**
```bash
# Generate SQL migration files from schema changes
npx drizzle-kit generate
# Apply pending migrations
npx drizzle-kit migrate
```
Generate creates timestamped SQL files in a `drizzle/` folder. You can review, edit, and version-control them. The `__drizzle_migrations` table tracks which have been applied. Best for: production, teams, CI/CD pipelines.

Key gotcha: `push` silently skips certain operations like custom GIN index operator classes and partial indexes. If your schema uses `sql` template literals for advanced index expressions, always verify with `\d+ tablename` after pushing. For production, always use `generate` + `migrate` — you get an auditable history and can add data migrations between schema changes.

title: Docker Compose networking — service discovery and custom networks
body:
Docker Compose creates a default bridge network where services can reach each other by service name. Understanding networking is essential for multi-container setups.

```yaml
# docker-compose.yml
services:
  api:
    build: ./api
    ports:
      - "3000:3000"  # host:container
    networks:
      - backend
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    environment:
      DATABASE_URL: postgresql://app:secret@db:5432/mydb  # 'db' resolves via DNS

  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    networks:
      - backend
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U app -d mydb"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    networks:
      - backend
      - cache

networks:
  backend:
    driver: bridge
  cache:
    driver: bridge

volumes:
  pgdata:
```

Service names (db, redis, api) become DNS hostnames within the same network. Services on different networks are isolated — `api` cannot reach `redis` unless they share a network. Port mapping (`3000:3000`) is only for host access; containers communicate on internal ports directly. The `depends_on` with `condition: service_healthy` ensures the database is ready before the API starts, replacing fragile `sleep` scripts.

title: Docker Compose healthchecks for PostgreSQL, Redis, and application services
body:
Healthchecks let Docker know when a container is genuinely ready, not just running. Without them, `depends_on` only waits for the container to start, not for the service inside to accept connections.

```yaml
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5
      start_period: 10s

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 3

  api:
    build: .
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1"]
      interval: 10s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3
      start_period: 15s
```

The `start_period` gives the service time to initialize before healthcheck failures count. For the API healthcheck, `curl` must be installed in the container image — if using a minimal image without curl, use `wget -q --spider http://localhost:3000/health` or write a custom script. Check container health with `docker inspect --format='{{.State.Health.Status}}' container_name`. Unhealthy containers are not automatically restarted unless you add `restart: unless-stopped`.

title: Docker layer caching — ordering Dockerfile instructions for fastest builds
body:
Docker caches each layer and reuses it if the instruction and all preceding layers are unchanged. Ordering instructions from least-frequently-changed to most-frequently-changed maximizes cache hits.

```dockerfile
# Base image — rarely changes
FROM node:20-alpine AS base
WORKDIR /app

# System deps — rarely changes
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat

# Package files — changes when deps change
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./

# Install deps — cached unless package files changed
RUN npm ci --production

# Source code — changes frequently, goes last
COPY . .

# Build
RUN npm run build
```

Common mistakes that bust the cache:
1. Copying everything before installing deps: `COPY . .` then `RUN npm ci` — every source change reinstalls all dependencies.
2. Using `ADD` instead of `COPY` — `ADD` has extra behavior (URL fetching, tar extraction) that can bypass caching.
3. Running `apt-get update && apt-get install` as separate RUN instructions — the update layer gets cached and becomes stale.

For monorepos, copy only the relevant package.json files first:
```dockerfile
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
COPY packages/api/package.json ./packages/api/
RUN npm ci --workspace=packages/api
COPY packages/api ./packages/api
```

Use `docker build --progress=plain` to see which layers are cached (`CACHED`) versus rebuilt.

title: GitHub Actions caching for npm, Docker, and Turborepo builds
body:
Caching in GitHub Actions drastically reduces CI time. Each cache type requires a different strategy.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      # npm cache — restores node_modules install time
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: 20
          cache: 'npm'  # built-in caching, keys off package-lock.json

      - run: npm ci

      # Turborepo remote cache (if using turbo)
      - run: npx turbo build --cache-dir=.turbo
        env:
          TURBO_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.TURBO_TOKEN }}
          TURBO_TEAM: ${{ vars.TURBO_TEAM }}

      # Docker layer cache for building images
      - uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          push: false
          cache-from: type=gha
          cache-to: type=gha,mode=max
```

GitHub Actions cache has a 10GB limit per repository. Caches not accessed in 7 days are evicted. The `actions/setup-node` cache option automatically caches the global npm/yarn/pnpm store. For Docker, `type=gha` uses GitHub Actions cache backend instead of registry-based caching, keeping everything within the repo's cache allocation. Set `mode=max` to cache all layers, not just the final image layers. For pnpm, use `cache: 'pnpm'` and ensure the lockfile is committed.

title: Railway vs Fly.io vs Render — platform comparison for full-stack apps
body:
All three platforms offer managed infrastructure for deploying web applications, but they differ in architecture, pricing, and developer experience.

**Railway:**
- Deploys from GitHub or Dockerfile. Automatic builds with Nixpacks.
- Managed PostgreSQL, Redis, and MySQL add-ons with one click.
- Usage-based pricing: $5/mo base + compute/memory per minute. No free tier (trial credits only).
- Internal networking via `service.railway.internal:port`.
- Best for: rapid prototyping, full-stack apps that need multiple services quickly.

**Fly.io:**
- Runs Docker containers on Firecracker microVMs worldwide.
- Supports persistent volumes, built-in Redis (Upstash), managed Postgres (via Supabase partnership).
- Pay for VM size: starts at ~$3/mo for shared-1x-256MB. Free allowance includes 3 shared VMs.
- Requires `fly.toml` config and `flyctl` CLI. Supports multi-region with read replicas.
- Best for: low-latency global deployments, long-running processes, WebSocket apps.

**Render:**
- Git-based deploys with auto-detect or Dockerfile.
- Managed PostgreSQL (free tier: 90-day expiry). Redis available.
- Free tier for web services (spins down after inactivity, 750 hours/mo). Paid starts at $7/mo.
- Supports background workers, cron jobs, static sites.
- Best for: projects that need a generous free tier, straightforward CRUD apps.

Key differentiator: Fly.io excels at multi-region; Railway excels at DX and speed; Render excels at free-tier availability.

title: Redis caching patterns — cache-aside, write-through, and write-behind
Structured logging with pino — context propagation and log levels
PostgreSQL CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY can leave invalid indexes
body:
When `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` fails or is cancelled, PostgreSQL leaves behind an invalid index that occupies space and silently degrades write performance without being used for queries.

```sql
-- This can fail if there are constraint violations or it's interrupted
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_email ON orders (email);

-- ERROR: could not create unique index "idx_orders_email"
-- DETAIL: Key (email)=(duplicate@test.com) is duplicated.
```

The invalid index remains and slows down every INSERT/UPDATE because PostgreSQL still maintains it. Check for invalid indexes:

```sql
SELECT indexrelid::regclass AS index_name,
       indrelid::regclass AS table_name,
       indisvalid
FROM pg_index
WHERE NOT indisvalid;
```

To fix, drop the invalid index and retry:
```sql
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_email;
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_orders_email ON orders (email);
```

Another gotcha: `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` cannot run inside a transaction block. If your migration tool wraps each file in a transaction (Drizzle's `migrate` does this), the command will fail with `ERROR: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction block`. You need to run concurrent index creation as a separate, non-transactional migration step.

title: PostgreSQL partial indexes to reduce index size and improve query speed
body:
A partial index only indexes rows matching a WHERE condition, making it smaller and faster than a full index for queries that always include that condition.

```sql
-- Full index on status: indexes ALL rows including completed orders
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders (status);
-- Size: ~50MB for 10M rows

-- Partial index: only indexes active orders (typically <5% of rows)
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_active ON orders (created_at)
  WHERE status IN ('pending', 'processing');
-- Size: ~2.5MB

-- Query that uses the partial index
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE status = 'pending'
  AND created_at > now() - interval '24 hours'
ORDER BY created_at;
```

The query planner uses the partial index when the query's WHERE clause implies the index condition. Another powerful use case — unique constraint only for non-deleted rows:

```sql
-- Allow multiple deleted users with the same email
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_email_active ON users (email)
  WHERE deleted_at IS NULL;
```

This replaces the common pattern of adding a `deleted_at` column to a unique constraint, which forces you to use a dummy timestamp for active rows. Partial indexes work with B-tree, GIN, GiST, and HNSW index types. Verify usage with EXPLAIN to confirm the planner selected your partial index.

title: PostgreSQL ERROR: canceling statement due to lock timeout
body:
This error occurs when a DDL statement cannot acquire the required lock within the configured `lock_timeout` period. It is intentional protection against blocking production traffic.

```
ERROR: canceling statement due to lock timeout
CONTEXT: SQL statement "ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN bio text"
```

Even `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` (which is fast) requires an `AccessExclusiveLock` briefly. If a long-running query holds an `AccessShareLock` on the table, the ALTER waits, and all subsequent queries queue behind it — causing a cascading outage.

Safe migration pattern:
```sql
-- Set aggressive timeouts in migration scripts
SET lock_timeout = '5s';
SET statement_timeout = '30s';

-- Attempt the migration
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN bio text;

-- If it fails, find what's blocking:
SELECT pid, query, state, wait_event_type, query_start
FROM pg_stat_activity
WHERE relation = 'users'::regclass
  AND pid != pg_backend_pid();

-- Kill the blocking query if appropriate
SELECT pg_cancel_backend(blocking_pid);
```

Always set `lock_timeout` in production migration scripts. Without it, an ALTER waiting for a lock will block all reads and writes on the table until either the blocking query finishes or you manually intervene. Tools like `pg-boss` and `graphile-worker` can retry failed migrations automatically.

title: PostgreSQL VACUUM and autovacuum — why dead tuples matter
body:
PostgreSQL uses MVCC, meaning UPDATE and DELETE don't physically remove old row versions (dead tuples). VACUUM reclaims this space. Without it, tables bloat and queries slow down.

```sql
-- Check dead tuple count per table
SELECT relname, n_live_tup, n_dead_tup,
       round(n_dead_tup::numeric / greatest(n_live_tup, 1) * 100, 1) AS dead_pct,
       last_vacuum, last_autovacuum
FROM pg_stat_user_tables
ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Tune autovacuum for a high-churn table
ALTER TABLE events SET (
  autovacuum_vacuum_threshold = 1000,        -- default 50
  autovacuum_vacuum_scale_factor = 0.05,      -- default 0.2 (20%)
  autovacuum_analyze_threshold = 500,
  autovacuum_analyze_scale_factor = 0.025
);
```

Autovacuum triggers when dead tuples exceed `threshold + scale_factor * table_size`. For a 10M row table with defaults, that's 50 + 0.2 * 10M = 2,000,050 dead tuples before vacuum runs — far too late. Lower `scale_factor` to 0.05 for large tables.

When a table is severely bloated, `VACUUM FULL` rewrites the entire table but takes an `AccessExclusiveLock` (blocks everything). For production, use `pg_repack` instead:
```bash
pg_repack --table events --no-superuser-check -d mydb
```

Monitor with: `SELECT pg_size_pretty(pg_total_relation_size('events'))` before and after.

title: Docker: container health check failing with exec format error
body:
When running multi-platform Docker images or using shell scripts in healthchecks, you may encounter this error:

```
OCI runtime exec failed: exec failed: unable to start container process:
exec: "/healthcheck.sh": exec format error
```

This happens for two reasons:

1. **Missing shebang**: The script lacks `#!/bin/sh` at the top, and Docker tries to execute it as a binary.
```dockerfile
# Wrong — no shebang
COPY healthcheck.sh /healthcheck.sh
HEALTHCHECK CMD /healthcheck.sh

# Fix — add shebang to healthcheck.sh
#!/bin/sh
curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1
```

2. **Architecture mismatch**: You built the image on arm64 (Apple Silicon) but run it on amd64, or vice versa. The healthcheck binary is for the wrong architecture.
```bash
# Check image architecture
docker inspect --format='{{.Architecture}}' myimage

# Build for the correct platform
docker build --platform linux/amd64 -t myimage .
```

3. **Windows line endings**: If the healthcheck script was created on Windows, `\r\n` line endings cause exec format error on Linux containers.
```bash
# Fix in Dockerfile
RUN sed -i 's/\r$//' /healthcheck.sh && chmod +x /healthcheck.sh
```

Safest approach — use CMD-SHELL form in docker-compose.yml to avoid script files entirely:
```yaml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD-SHELL", "curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1"]
```

title: GitHub Actions — reusable workflow for Docker build, test, and deploy
body:
A well-structured CI pipeline caches aggressively, runs tests in parallel, and only deploys on the main branch.

```yaml
# .github/workflows/ci.yml
name: CI/CD Pipeline
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  lint-and-typecheck:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 20, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run lint && npm run typecheck

  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:16-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
          POSTGRES_DB: testdb
        ports: ['5432:5432']
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-timeout 3s
          --health-retries 5
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with: { node-version: 20, cache: npm }
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:test@localhost:5432/testdb

  deploy:
    needs: [lint-and-typecheck, test]
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: echo "Deploy to production here"
```

The `services` block spins up a real PostgreSQL container for integration tests. Jobs run in parallel by default — `deploy` uses `needs` to wait for both checks. Use `if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'` to skip deploy on pull requests.

title: Docker Compose depends_on does not wait for service readiness without healthcheck
body:
A common mistake is assuming `depends_on` ensures the dependent service is ready to accept connections. Without a healthcheck condition, it only waits for the container to start.

```yaml
# WRONG — API starts before Postgres is ready to accept connections
services:
  api:
    build: .
    depends_on:
      - db  # only waits for container to start, NOT for PG to be ready
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine

# RIGHT — waits for Postgres healthcheck to pass
services:
  api:
    build: .
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
  db:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5
```

Without the healthcheck, your application will crash on startup with:
```
Error: connect ECONNREFUSED 172.18.0.2:5432
```

This was especially confusing because Docker Compose v2 changed the default behavior. In Compose v1 (docker-compose), `depends_on` with conditions was a v2.1 file format feature. In Compose v2 (docker compose — no hyphen), conditions work natively. The `service_healthy` condition requires that the dependency has a `healthcheck` defined, otherwise Docker Compose will error with: `service "db" has no healthcheck configured`.

Alternative if you cannot add healthchecks: add retry logic in your application's database connection code with exponential backoff.

title: PostgreSQL expression indexes for computed column queries
Redis cache invalidation strategies for consistency with PostgreSQL
PostgreSQL SKIP LOCKED for reliable job queue processing
body:
SKIP LOCKED lets multiple workers dequeue jobs without blocking each other, making PostgreSQL a viable job queue for moderate workloads.

```sql
-- Create jobs table
CREATE TABLE jobs (
  id bigserial PRIMARY KEY,
  queue text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'default',
  payload jsonb NOT NULL,
  status text NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending',
  run_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  locked_by text,
  locked_at timestamptz,
  attempts int NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
  max_attempts int NOT NULL DEFAULT 3,
  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE INDEX idx_jobs_pending ON jobs (run_at)
  WHERE status = 'pending';

-- Worker dequeue query (each worker runs this)
WITH next_job AS (
  SELECT id FROM jobs
  WHERE status = 'pending'
    AND run_at <= now()
    AND attempts < max_attempts
  ORDER BY run_at
  LIMIT 1
  FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED
)
UPDATE jobs SET
  status = 'running',
  locked_by = 'worker-1',
  locked_at = now(),
  attempts = attempts + 1
FROM next_job
WHERE jobs.id = next_job.id
RETURNING *;
```

`FOR UPDATE` locks the selected row. `SKIP LOCKED` tells other workers to skip already-locked rows instead of waiting. This eliminates contention between workers. The CTE + UPDATE is atomic — no race conditions. Add a `run_at` column for delayed jobs and scheduled retries. For failed jobs, set `status = 'pending'` and `run_at = now() + interval '30 seconds' * attempts` for exponential backoff. This pattern handles ~1000 jobs/sec; for higher throughput, use a dedicated queue like pgBoss or BullMQ.

title: PostgreSQL: adding a NOT NULL column with DEFAULT locks the table in PG < 11
body:
In PostgreSQL 10 and earlier, adding a column with a DEFAULT value rewrites the entire table, holding an AccessExclusiveLock for the duration. On large tables, this can cause minutes of downtime.

```sql
-- PG 10: rewrites entire table, blocks ALL queries
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN priority int NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
-- On a 50M row table, this can take 5+ minutes

-- PG 11+: instant operation, default stored in catalog
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN priority int NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
-- Completes in milliseconds regardless of table size
```

PostgreSQL 11 changed this behavior: the default value is stored in `pg_attribute.attmissingval` and applied at read time. Existing rows are not physically rewritten. However, there are exceptions even in PG 11+:

```sql
-- These STILL rewrite the table even in PG 16:
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMN computed int NOT NULL DEFAULT random();
-- Volatile functions require row-by-row evaluation

ALTER TABLE orders ALTER COLUMN status TYPE varchar(50);
-- Changing column type rewrites the table (unless cast is binary-compatible)
```

Check your PostgreSQL version before planning migrations: `SELECT version();`. If you're on PG 10 or earlier, the safe approach is: add nullable column, backfill in batches, add NOT NULL constraint with NOT VALID, then validate. If you're on PG 11+, `ADD COLUMN ... NOT NULL DEFAULT constant` is safe for production.

title: Setting up pgvector with Drizzle ORM for semantic search
Docker: no space left on device — cleaning up unused resources
body:
Docker accumulates images, containers, volumes, and build cache over time. When disk fills up, you'll see:

```
Error response from daemon: failed to mkdir: mkdir /var/lib/docker/overlay2/...
no space left on device
```

Or during builds:
```
failed to copy: write /var/lib/docker/overlay2/.../diff/app/node_modules/...
no space left on device
```

Diagnose:
```bash
# See Docker disk usage breakdown
docker system df

# Typical output:
# TYPE            TOTAL   ACTIVE  SIZE      RECLAIMABLE
# Images          45      3       12.4GB    11.2GB (90%)
# Containers      12      2       1.2GB     800MB (66%)
# Build Cache     -       -       8.5GB     8.5GB
# Volumes         15      4       3.2GB     2.1GB (65%)
```

Clean up:
```bash
# Remove all stopped containers, unused networks, dangling images, and build cache
docker system prune

# Also remove all unused images (not just dangling) — recovers the most space
docker system prune -a

# Remove unused volumes (careful — this deletes database data!)
docker volume prune

# Nuclear option: remove everything
docker system prune -a --volumes
```

Preventive measures: add `--rm` to `docker run` commands so containers auto-delete. Use `.dockerignore` to exclude `node_modules`, `.git`, and other large directories from build context. Set up a cron job: `0 3 * * 0 docker system prune -af --filter 'until=168h'` to weekly remove resources older than 7 days.

title: PostgreSQL connection pooling — PgBouncer vs application-level pooling
body:
PostgreSQL creates a new process for each connection, consuming ~5-10MB of memory. At 200+ connections, the overhead significantly impacts performance. Connection pooling is essential.

**PgBouncer (external pooler):**
```ini
# pgbouncer.ini
[databases]
mydb = host=localhost port=5432 dbname=mydb

[pgbouncer]
listen_port = 6432
pool_mode = transaction    # recommended for most apps
max_client_conn = 1000     # clients connecting to PgBouncer
default_pool_size = 20     # actual PG connections per database
min_pool_size = 5
reserve_pool_size = 5
reserve_pool_timeout = 3
```

Pool modes:
- `session`: connection assigned per client session (safest, least efficient)
- `transaction`: connection assigned per transaction (best balance)
- `statement`: connection assigned per statement (breaks multi-statement transactions)

**Application-level pooling (node-postgres):**
```typescript
import { Pool } from 'pg';
const pool = new Pool({
  connectionString: process.env.DATABASE_URL,
  max: 20,           // max connections in pool
  idleTimeoutMillis: 30000,
  connectionTimeoutMillis: 5000,
});
```

When to use which: PgBouncer when you have multiple application instances (each would create its own pool). Application-level pooling when you have a single instance. For serverless (Vercel, Lambda), use a managed pooler like Neon's or Supabase's built-in PgBouncer — serverless functions can spawn hundreds of connections per cold start.

title: GitHub Actions — matrix strategy for testing across Node versions and databases
body:
Matrix builds run your test suite against multiple environments in parallel, catching compatibility issues early.

```yaml
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    strategy:
      fail-fast: false  # don't cancel other jobs if one fails
      matrix:
        node-version: [18, 20, 22]
        postgres-version: [15, 16]
        exclude:
          - node-version: 18
            postgres-version: 15  # skip this combination

    services:
      postgres:
        image: postgres:${{ matrix.postgres-version }}-alpine
        env:
          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test
          POSTGRES_DB: testdb
        ports: ['5432:5432']
        options: >-
          --health-cmd pg_isready
          --health-interval 5s
          --health-retries 5

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }}
          cache: npm
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm test
        env:
          DATABASE_URL: postgresql://postgres:test@localhost:5432/testdb
          NODE_ENV: test

    name: Node ${{ matrix.node-version }} / PG ${{ matrix.postgres-version }}
```

This creates 5 parallel jobs (3 x 2 minus 1 exclusion). Set `fail-fast: false` so a failure in Node 18 doesn't cancel the Node 20 and 22 runs — you want to see all results. The `name` field makes the GitHub UI show descriptive job names instead of generic indices. Use `include` to add one-off combinations with extra environment variables. Each matrix job gets its own PostgreSQL service container, so tests don't interfere with each other.

title: Redis sorted sets for rate limiting with sliding window
Setting up Redis caching layer with cache-aside pattern in Next.js
PostgreSQL: column cannot be cast automatically to type — migration error
body:
When changing a column type, PostgreSQL requires an explicit cast if the conversion is not binary-compatible:

```
ERROR: column "price" cannot be cast automatically to type integer
HINT: You might need to specify "USING price::integer".
```

This happens when running migrations that change column types:
```sql
-- Fails: text -> integer has no implicit cast
ALTER TABLE products ALTER COLUMN price TYPE integer;

-- Fix: provide explicit USING clause
ALTER TABLE products ALTER COLUMN price TYPE integer USING price::integer;

-- For more complex conversions:
ALTER TABLE products ALTER COLUMN price TYPE integer
  USING (CASE WHEN price ~ '^[0-9]+$' THEN price::integer ELSE 0 END);

-- varchar(255) -> text: this works without USING (binary compatible)
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN name TYPE text;

-- text -> varchar(50): works but truncates silently if data exceeds 50 chars
ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN name TYPE varchar(50);
-- Better: check first
SELECT max(length(name)) FROM users;
```

Important: `ALTER COLUMN TYPE` rewrites the entire table, holding an AccessExclusiveLock. For large tables in production, this causes downtime. The zero-downtime alternative is:
1. Add a new column with the target type
2. Backfill data in batches
3. Swap columns using rename

Drizzle's `generate` will produce `ALTER COLUMN TYPE` statements when you change a column type in your schema. Always review generated migrations before applying to production. Some safe casts that don't rewrite: `varchar(N)` to `varchar(M)` where M > N, `varchar(N)` to `text`, `integer` to `bigint`.

title: Docker multi-stage build with test stage for CI pipelines
body:
A multi-stage Dockerfile can include a test stage that CI runs without building the final production image, saving time when tests fail early.

```dockerfile
# Stage 1: Dependencies
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci

# Stage 2: Test (CI targets this stage)
FROM deps AS test
COPY . .
RUN npm run typecheck
RUN npm run lint
CMD ["npm", "test"]

# Stage 3: Build (only runs if tests pass)
FROM deps AS build
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 4: Production
FROM node:20-alpine AS production
WORKDIR /app
RUN addgroup -S app && adduser -S app -G app
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY package.json ./
USER app
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]
```

Usage in CI:
```bash
# Run tests only (stops at test stage)
docker build --target test -t myapp:test .
docker run --rm myapp:test

# Build production image (skips test stage, goes deps -> build -> production)
docker build --target production -t myapp:latest .
```

The `--target` flag tells Docker to stop at a specific stage. The production stage copies from `build` and `deps` but never references `test`, so the test stage's layers are not included in the production image. The non-root `app` user in the production stage follows security best practices. Layer caching means if only test files changed, the `deps` stage is cached.

title: PostgreSQL: BRIN indexes for time-series and append-only data
body:
BRIN (Block Range Index) indexes store summary information about ranges of physical table blocks. They are extremely small and efficient for naturally ordered data like timestamps in append-only tables.

```sql
-- Perfect use case: time-series events table where rows are inserted in order
CREATE TABLE events (
  id bigserial PRIMARY KEY,
  created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
  event_type text NOT NULL,
  payload jsonb
);

-- BRIN index: ~1000x smaller than equivalent B-tree
CREATE INDEX idx_events_created_brin ON events
  USING brin (created_at)
  WITH (pages_per_range = 32);

-- Compare sizes on a 50M row table:
-- B-tree on created_at: ~1.1GB
-- BRIN on created_at: ~300KB
```

BRIN works by storing min/max values for each range of 32 (configurable) consecutive pages. When querying `WHERE created_at > '2025-01-01'`, PostgreSQL skips entire block ranges where the max value is before the threshold.

When BRIN works well:
- Data is physically ordered by the indexed column (INSERT order matches sort order)
- Table is large (millions of rows)
- Queries filter on ranges, not exact values

When BRIN fails:
```sql
-- Random updates destroy physical ordering
UPDATE events SET created_at = now() WHERE id = old_id;
-- After many updates, BRIN becomes useless because block ranges
-- contain mixed timestamps
```

Check physical correlation with: `SELECT correlation FROM pg_stats WHERE tablename = 'events' AND attname = 'created_at';`. A value close to 1.0 or -1.0 means BRIN will be effective. Values near 0 mean the data is randomly ordered and BRIN won't help.

title: Railway deployment — Dockerfile with health checks and internal networking
body:
Railway deploys Docker containers and provides managed PostgreSQL and Redis. Configuring health checks, internal networking, and environment variables correctly is essential.

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY package.json package-lock.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
RUN apk add --no-cache wget
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
COPY --from=build /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY package.json ./

ENV PORT=3000
EXPOSE 3000

HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=5s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget -q --spider http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1

CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]
```

Railway configuration via `railway.toml`:
```toml
[build]
builder = "dockerfile"
dockerfilePath = "Dockerfile"

[deploy]
healthcheckPath = "/health"
healthcheckTimeout = 300
restartPolicyType = "on_failure"
restartPolicyMaxRetries = 3
```

Internal networking between Railway services:
```typescript
// Use internal URLs for service-to-service communication
const REDIS_URL = process.env.REDIS_PRIVATE_URL;  // redis://default:pass@redis.railway.internal:6379
const DB_URL = process.env.DATABASE_PRIVATE_URL;   // postgresql://...@postgres.railway.internal:5432/railway

// External URL for client-facing traffic
const PUBLIC_URL = process.env.RAILWAY_PUBLIC_DOMAIN; // myapp-production.up.railway.app
```

Always use `*_PRIVATE_URL` for inter-service communication — it routes through Railway's internal network, avoiding egress charges and reducing latency. The public URL goes through Railway's proxy with TLS termination.

title: Fly.io: volume data loss when scaling to zero or redeploying
body:
Fly.io volumes are attached to specific machines and are NOT replicated. If a machine is destroyed during redeployment, the volume data can be lost.

```toml
# fly.toml
[mounts]
  source = "myapp_data"
  destination = "/data"
```

Dangerous scenarios:
1. **Scaling to zero** with `fly scale count 0` destroys machines but keeps volumes. Scaling back up creates new machines that may not get the old volumes.
2. **Redeployment** with `fly deploy` creates new machines and migrates volumes, but if the deploy fails mid-way, you can end up with orphaned volumes.
3. **Multi-region deployments** — volumes are region-locked. A volume in `ord` (Chicago) cannot be accessed by a machine in `lhr` (London).

```bash
# Check volume status
fly volumes list

# Volumes show which machine they're attached to:
# ID                   STATE   NAME        SIZE  REGION  MACHINE         CREATED AT
# vol_abc123           created myapp_data  1GB   ord     d8901234        2025-01-01

# Fork (snapshot) a volume before risky operations
fly volumes fork vol_abc123
```

Best practices: do NOT use Fly volumes for PostgreSQL data — use Fly Postgres (managed) or an external database. Use volumes only for ephemeral caches or file uploads that are also stored in object storage. Always `fly volumes fork` before destructive operations. For SQLite on Fly, use LiteFS for replication across machines.

title: Render deployment — auto-deploy from GitHub with build and health checks
body:
Render connects directly to GitHub repositories and auto-deploys on push. Configuration is done via `render.yaml` (Infrastructure as Code) or the dashboard.

```yaml
# render.yaml — Blueprint for entire infrastructure
services:
  - type: web
    name: api
    runtime: node
    buildCommand: npm ci && npm run build
    startCommand: node dist/index.js
    healthCheckPath: /health
    envVars:
      - key: DATABASE_URL
        fromDatabase:
          name: mydb
          property: connectionString
      - key: REDIS_URL
        fromService:
          name: cache
          type: redis
          property: connectionString
      - key: NODE_ENV
        value: production
    autoDeploy: true
    plan: starter  # $7/mo, no sleep

  - type: worker
    name: job-processor
    runtime: node
    buildCommand: npm ci && npm run build
    startCommand: node dist/worker.js
    envVars:
      - key: DATABASE_URL
        fromDatabase:
          name: mydb
          property: connectionString

databases:
  - name: mydb
    plan: starter  # $7/mo, 1GB storage
    postgresMajorVersion: 16
```

Key behaviors:
- Free web services spin down after 15 minutes of inactivity (50-second cold start).
- `healthCheckPath` must return 200 within the timeout or Render rolls back the deploy.
- Environment variables from `fromDatabase` and `fromService` are auto-populated when services are created together.
- Render does not support Docker Compose — use `render.yaml` to define multi-service architectures.
- Build caching is automatic: Render caches `node_modules` between deploys if `package-lock.json` hasn't changed.
- Preview environments: enable per-PR deploys in settings for staging-like environments on each pull request.

title: Redis pub/sub vs Streams — choosing the right messaging pattern
Drizzle ORM — generating and applying migrations with custom SQL
body:
Drizzle's migration workflow lets you generate SQL from schema changes and inject custom SQL for operations the schema DSL cannot express.

```bash
# Generate migration from schema diff
npx drizzle-kit generate
# Creates: drizzle/0001_add_search_vector.sql
```

The generated SQL might look like:
```sql
ALTER TABLE "articles" ADD COLUMN "search_vector" tsvector;
```

But you need a GIN index and trigger — add a custom migration:
```bash
# Create a custom migration file
touch drizzle/0002_custom_search_index.sql
```

```sql
-- drizzle/0002_custom_search_index.sql
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_articles_search
  ON articles USING gin (search_vector);

CREATE FUNCTION articles_search_update() RETURNS trigger AS $$
BEGIN
  NEW.search_vector :=
    setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(NEW.title, '')), 'A') ||
    setweight(to_tsvector('english', coalesce(NEW.body, '')), 'B');
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

CREATE TRIGGER trg_articles_search
  BEFORE INSERT OR UPDATE ON articles
  FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION articles_search_update();
```

Apply migrations programmatically:
```typescript
import { drizzle } from 'drizzle-orm/node-postgres';
import { migrate } from 'drizzle-orm/node-postgres/migrator';

const db = drizzle(process.env.DATABASE_URL!);
await migrate(db, { migrationsFolder: './drizzle' });
```

Gotcha: `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` cannot run inside a transaction. Drizzle's migrator wraps each file in a transaction by default. You must run concurrent index migrations outside the migrator, as a separate step in your deploy script.
